message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>SARS-CoV-2 transmission in Sub-Saharan Africa has probably been underestimated. Population-based seroprevalence studies are needed to determine the extent of transmission in the continent.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Blood samples from a cohort of Gambian pregnant women were tested for SARS-CoV-2 total IgM/IgG before (
                    <jats:italic>Pre-pandemic1:</jats:italic>
                    October-December 2019 and
                    <jats:italic>Pre-pandemic2:</jats:italic>
                    February-June 2020) and during the pandemic (
                    <jats:italic>Post-wave1</jats:italic>
                    : October-December 2020,
                    <jats:italic>Post-wave2:</jats:italic>
                    May-June 2021; and
                    <jats:italic>Post-wave3</jats:italic>
                    : October-December 2021). Samples positive for total SARS-CoV-2 IgM/IgG were tested for protein-specific antibodies.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    SARS-CoV-2 total IgM/IgG seroprevalence was 0.9% 95%CI (0.2, 4.9) in
                    <jats:italic>Pre-pandemic1</jats:italic>
                    ; 4.1% (1.4, 11.4) in
                    <jats:italic>Pre-pandemic2</jats:italic>
                    ; 31.1% (25.2, 37.7) in
                    <jats:italic>Post-wave1</jats:italic>
                    ; 62.5% (55.8, 68.8) in
                    <jats:italic>Post-wave2</jats:italic>
                    and 90.0% (85.1, 93.5) in
                    <jats:italic>Post-wave3.</jats:italic>
                    S-protein IgG and NCP-protein IgG seroprevalence also increased at each
                    <jats:italic>Post-wave</jats:italic>
                    period. Although S-protein IgG and NCP-protein IgG seroprevalence was similar at
                    <jats:italic>Post-wave1</jats:italic>
                    , S-protein IgG seroprevalence was higher at
                    <jats:italic>Post-wave2</jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>Post-wave3</jats:italic>
                    , [prevalence difference (PD) 13.5 (0.1, 26.8) and prevalence ratio (PR) 1.5 (1.0, 2.3) in
                    <jats:italic>Post-wave2</jats:italic>
                    ; and 22.9 (9.2, 36.6) and 1.4 (1.1, 1.8) in
                    <jats:italic>Post-wave3</jats:italic>
                    respectively, p<0.001].
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>SARS-CoV-2 transmission in The Gambia during the first three COVID-19 waves was high, differing significantly from official numbers of COVID-19 cases reported. Our findings are important for policy makers in managing the near-endemic COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Highlights</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>High specificity of the IgM/IgG SARS-CoV-2 test using samples collected prepandemic</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Very high (>90%) SARS-CoV-2 seroprevalence after third COVID-19 wave in The Gambia</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>High SARS-CoV-2 transmission contrasts with low number of COVID-19 reported cases</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: SARS-CoV-2 seroprevalence in pregnant women during the first three COVID-19 waves in The Gambia

reference.article-title: On the origin and evolution of SARS-CoV-2
